Q1: I tried to keep things steady, you know, being 29 and having other responsibilities. I usually aimed to study in the evenings after work, maybe three or four times a week for a couple hours each time. I'm a hands-on learner, so I'd try to work through problems practically rather than just reading. But honestly, my routine wasn't as consistent as I wanted it to be. Some weeks I managed well, others I barely touched the material.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied more than I'd like to admit. I tried to keep regular evening sessions, but when deadlines approached or work got busy, everything shifted. Sometimes I'd cram a lot into weekends when I had more breathing room. The intention was always to be steady, but life doesn't always cooperate with your plans.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or seeing if any deadlines were coming up. Sometimes I'd get an email notification and that would remind me to log in. I wasn't someone who browsed around the LMS just to explore—I went in with a purpose, got what I needed, and logged out. It felt more like a tool than a place to hang out.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, I did get into a rhythm where I'd check the LMS every Monday and Thursday evening. It became almost automatic—finish dinner, make some tea, open the laptop. But that routine broke down when I hit some rough patches emotionally. Once I fell out of that habit, I never quite got back into it the same way.
Q5: The video lectures were actually helpful when I could focus on them. I learn better by seeing things done, so watching someone work through problems step by step made more sense than reading about it. The discussion posts were occasionally useful too, when other students asked questions I hadn't thought of. But overall, I needed more interactive, hands-on stuff that the LMS didn't really provide.
Q6: I mostly ignored the supplementary readings and some of the optional quizzes. The readings felt too passive for how I learn—I'd read them but nothing would stick. The optional quizzes seemed like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the required stuff. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see how it connected to the actual assessments or real-world application. If I could work through something with my hands or apply it practically, that felt valuable. But if it was just reading theory without any clear purpose, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to feel like I was building toward something concrete.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're already behind and feeling low on motivation, optional stuff is the first thing to go. Required activities had this weight to them—I had to do them or face consequences. Optional ones were easy to push aside even when I knew they might help me understand better.
Q9: Yes, quite a bit actually. I'd download the lecture slides and PDFs, then work through them on paper at my kitchen table. I also used a physical notebook where I'd write out problems and solutions by hand—that's how I process information best. Sometimes I'd watch YouTube videos explaining similar concepts because I needed a different perspective than what the course offered.
Q10: There were many nights where I sat with my notebook, working through practice problems I'd printed out earlier. The LMS wouldn't show any of that—it just looked like I wasn't doing anything. I also did a lot of thinking and mental review during my commute to work. Processing information doesn't always happen in front of a screen.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once, maybe twice, then download the slides and never go back to the video itself. I'd work from those slides for days, adding my own notes, trying problems on paper. The LMS probably thought I only engaged with that material briefly, but I was actually returning to it repeatedly in my own way.
Q12: There was a stretch where I felt really sad and disconnected from everything, not just the course. Getting out of bed felt hard, let alone engaging with complex material. My motivation was already low, and when that emotional weight hit, the course just felt impossible. I did some mindfulness exercises they suggested, but it didn't magically fix things. I kept showing up, but barely.
Q13: Work was demanding, and by the time I got home, I was often mentally exhausted. The fatigue made it hard to focus on dense material. When I did log into the LMS, I'd sometimes just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely meant the course got less of my best energy—it got whatever was left over.
Q14: Many times, unfortunately. I'd open a lecture video and let it play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through the discussion board without really reading what anyone wrote. My body was present, but my mind was checked out. It's frustrating because the time was spent, but nothing was gained.
Q15: I postponed the more challenging assignments repeatedly, even knowing they were important. Part of it was fear of failing at them—easier to avoid than to try and confirm I didn't understand. Part of it was just low energy and motivation making everything feel harder than it should. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow became next week.
Q16: Deadlines were really the main thing that got me moving. Without a deadline, I'd drift and procrastinate endlessly. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly find the energy to log in, watch videos at double speed, and rush through assignments. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only system that worked for me given my low motivation.
Q17: Feedback was sparse and often came too late to really help me adjust. When I did get feedback, I'd try to incorporate it, but by then I'd already moved on to the next thing. If feedback had been quicker and more specific, maybe I would have engaged differently. As it was, it felt like shouting into a void most of the time.
Q18: The structure was fairly linear, which I appreciated in some ways—I knew what came next. But it also felt rigid and didn't accommodate different learning styles well. I needed more hands-on activities built into the structure, not just videos and readings. The pace assumed everyone was a full-time student, which I wasn't.
Q19: The lack of interactive, practical exercises made engagement harder for me as a kinesthetic learner. Everything was watch-this, read-that, which doesn't suit how I process information. What would have helped is more simulations or practice problems built directly into the LMS. The discussion forums tried to create interaction, but they felt forced rather than organic.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, trying to engage but not quite managing to produce finished work. Maybe they're confused about what's expected, or they're struggling with the material but don't know how to ask for help. High activity doesn't mean high understanding. Sometimes it means high confusion.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline that never showed up in the system. If someone looked only at my LMS data, they'd miss all the notebook work and external studying I did. My exam score was still poor, but my understanding was probably better than the login data suggested. The system can't see everything.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, how long something was open. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention or if your mind was elsewhere. They can't capture learning that happens offline, or the emotional state you're in while engaging. It's like measuring the shadow of something instead of the thing itself.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting real mental effort into understanding it. It's not just going through the motions—it's being curious, asking questions, struggling with problems until they make sense. For me, engagement also means doing things with my hands, applying concepts practically. It's an active state, not passive consumption.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting just to keep going despite feeling sad and unmotivated. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with my notebook away from the screen, or the mental effort of trying to stay resilient when everything felt heavy. It also wouldn't show that I genuinely wanted to succeed—I just couldn't always translate that desire into visible action.
